quotes.verbatim_quote:
Blues progressions form the basis for so much music now in existence that a knowledge of them if
very important to the well-rounded musician.

in this book, it refers to any 12 bar (measure)
Progression.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines 'blues' as a 12-bar form for the book.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
When choosing the inversions, pay particular attention to the melody note (the highest pitch — the
top note) in each chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Inversion selection rule based on melody line.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Actually, you should
be watching for smooth voice leading (as was discussed in section 13) wherever possible.
Some progressions cannot be played with a pick only; you must use your fingers
Most of these progressions are among the accepted ones used by musicians who play blues.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Frames the catalog as standard practice.

quotes.verbatim_quote: the blues sound is usually associated with the dominant 7th family.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Identifies dominant 7 family as the blues color.

quotes.topic: I in bar 1, IV in bar 5
quotes.verbatim_quote:
in 99.9999% of all cases, some form of [ chord will be in bar lto
start the progression, and some form of a IV chord will be in bar 5.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Defines the structural skeleton of the 12-bar.

quotes.verbatim_quote: This progression has a much bluesier sound to most ears due to the abundance of 9th chords
Do anything that sounds good to you, but try to understand why you are doing it.
